SEARCH
Package com.tngtech.archunit.core.domain

Class AccessTarget.FieldAccessTarget

java.lang.Object
com.tngtech.archunit.core.domain.AccessTarget
com.tngtech.archunit.core.domain.AccessTarget.FieldAccessTarget
All Implemented Interfaces:
HasDescription, CanBeAnnotated, HasName, HasName.AndFullName, HasOwner<JavaClass>, HasType
Enclosing class:
AccessTarget
@PublicAPI(usage=ACCESS) public static final class AccessTarget.FieldAccessTarget extends AccessTarget implements HasType
Represents an AccessTarget where the target is a field. For further elaboration about the necessity to distinguish FieldAccessTarget from JavaField, refer to the documentation at AccessTarget.

  • Method Details

    • getType

      @PublicAPI(usage=ACCESS) public JavaType getType()
      Specified by:
      getType in interface HasType
      Returns:
      The (possibly generic) JavaType of this object. Refer to the documentation of JavaType for further information.
      See Also:
      • HasType.getRawType()

    • getRawType

      @PublicAPI(usage=ACCESS) public JavaClass getRawType()
      Specified by:
      getRawType in interface HasType
      Returns:
      The raw type of this object. This is effectively the same as calling HasType.getType().toErasure(). E.g. given a JavaParameterizedType java.util.List<String> the raw type (i.e. type erasure) would be the JavaClass java.util.List.
      See Also:
      • HasType.getType()

    • resolveMember

      @PublicAPI(usage=ACCESS) public Optional<JavaField> resolveMember()
      Attempts to resolve an imported field that has the same type and name as this target.
      The result will be the only accessible field that the origin can reach. I.e. if there are multiple fields with the same type and name in the hierarchy of the target, then the one that is accessible will be picked. This must be unique, otherwise the compiler would force disambiguating by an explicit cast. Consider e.g. 
      
 interface A {
     Class<?> value = A.class;
 }

 interface B {
     Class<?> value = B.class;
 }

 class C implements A, B {
 }

 class X {
     C c;
     // ...
     Class<?> origin() {
         return ((B) c).value;
     }
 }
      Without the cast ((B) c) this code would not compile because the field reference is ambiguous.
      Note that the result can still be Optional.empty() in case the class containing the field has not been imported.
      Overrides:
      resolveMember in class AccessTarget
      Returns:
      The field that matches this target, or Optional.empty() if no matching field was imported.
      See Also:
      • AccessTarget.MethodCallTarget.resolveMember()
      • resolveMember()
      • AccessTarget.ConstructorCallTarget.resolveMember()

    • getDescription

      @PublicAPI(usage=ACCESS) public String getDescription()
